lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a normalized repository version of University of Michigan CME item AJF7399.0001.001, a 2006 uncorrected OCR/Level-1 encoding of H. R. Bramley's 1884 English Psalter edition. U-M's 578-image sequence represents an unidentified printed copy from its title page through the glossary but is physically partial; the independently cached 624-page Robarts scan represents one more complete bound copy. Bramley's Middle English reading text uses University College MS 64 (U) as late-selected base, including later U-supply leaves; his Latin text and selected readings pass through a complete working transcript of Sidney Sussex MS 89 (S); Laud Misc. 286 (L) supplies the sixty-line metrical preface and six finite gaps. Eleven additional manuscripts enter at specimen or selected-apparatus scope. Direct inspection corrects three OCR-corrupted specimen headings to Tanner 1, Laud Misc. 321, and Magdalen College MS 52; one printed B 469 apparatus reference remains unresolved. Eleven pinned Oxford TEI catalog expressions, James's Sidney catalog, two scholarly articles, selected manuscript images, access routes, and rights layers are modeled separately. Public catalog access never implies manuscript-image access, and provider-inventory completeness never implies complete physical-carrier coverage.

catalogingSubject.label: English Psalter
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Richard Rolle's one medieval prose work comprising a Latin-lemma Psalter and seven Canticles with Middle English verse-by-verse translation and Middle English prose commentary. The repository XML represents Bramley's 1884 edition and also retains a separate anonymous sixty-line Middle English metrical preface as zero-item edition paratext. The 150 Psalm divisions and seven Canticle divisions are sections of the one Rolle work, not 157 independent works. The physical manuscripts, Bramley edition, U-M encoding, page-image carriers, and repository XML remain separate lineage layers.

assertion.notes: The secure terminus is Rolle's death in 1349; this is not a claim that composition occurred in 1349.

Bramley infers this range from a reference after Walter Stapledon's death on 14 October 1326 and an apparent absence of reference to Edward II's murder on 21 September 1327. The range is deliberately not normalized as a confirmed discovery facet.
This is the publication date of the source edition, not necessarily the first historical publication of any passage.

extent.description: One Rolle prose work: one prose prologue, 150 Psalm divisions with 2,612 verse/comment units, and seven Canticle divisions with 168 units. One additional zero-item anonymous metrical preface supplies one verse group and sixty verse lines as edition paratext. Thus the 159 section count is one paratext DIV2 + 158 Rolle-work DIV2 divisions. The raw XML has four DIV1, 159 DIV2, 2,780 DIV3, 2,786 P, 1 LG, 60 L, 1,286 count-once apparatus NOTE1 elements, 578 PB, and 579 EPB elements. The verse metric records representation extent only and does not alter the work's prose form or one-item count.

formStatements.description: The cataloged Rolle work is prose: Latin lemmata and Middle English translations are followed by continuous Middle English exposition. The separate anonymous sixty-line verse preface is zero-item edition paratext and does not make the work mixed-form.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The prologue, verse-by-verse translations, prose commentary, and anonymous metrical preface are Middle English.

scope.description: Latin Psalm and Canticle lemmata embedded throughout Rolle's work

notes: The 150 Psalms and seven Canticles are divisions of one Rolle work, not 157 works.
The anonymous sixty-line metrical preface is zero-item edition paratext. Its lines remain in representation metrics but do not change the cataloged work's prose form.
No normalized composition place is asserted. Witness production places, dialect regions, holdings, and Rolle's Yorkshire associations remain distinct.

Bramley's 1884 edition is still the standard edition of the original version according to Kuczynski, but it is not a modern critical edition and represents a historically layered U/S/L editorial construction.

Kuczynski's census of 25 original-version manuscripts, including four fragments or extracts, and 19 revision manuscripts describes the wider tradition rather than the number of direct sources for ajf7399.

next_steps: Treat 1326-1327 only as Bramley's conjecture unless stronger current source analysis confirms it.
Do not convert Rolle's Yorkshire association or Northern dialect into a normalized composition place.

assertion.notes: Authorship covers Rolle's Middle English exposition, not the underlying biblical Latin or the anonymous metrical preface.
Rolle translates the Latin Psalm and Canticle lemmata into Middle English verse by verse.
The sixty-line metrical preface is separate edition paratext copied from Laud Misc. 286 and is not by Rolle on the evidence inspected.
Bramley's responsibility belongs to the 1884 edition represented by the XML, not to the abstract medieval work.

scope.description: Edition glossary and dialect/base-text advice; the glossary is omitted from continuous XML transcription

assertion.notes: The 2006 U-M Level-1 TEI text was produced by uncorrected OCR linked to page images and was not content-edited.

summaries.value: Rolle presents each Latin Psalm or Canticle verse, translates it into Middle English, and expounds it in Middle English prose. The commentary combines literal explanation, moral instruction, Christological reading, and contemplative devotion. The work includes all 150 Psalms and seven Canticles and is preceded by Rolle's prose prologue.

The XML represents Bramley's title page, an anonymous sixty-line verse preface on printed pages 1-2, Rolle's prose work on pages 3-526, and 1,286 count-once apparatus notes. It retains page milestones and omission placeholders rather than continuous transcription for most introductory matter and the glossary on pages 527-556. U-M sequence 23-548 and Robarts PDF 31-556 are the encoded target run including paratext; the Rolle work itself is U-M sequence 25-548 and PDF 33-556.

assertion.notes: Kuczynski explicitly uses English Psalter as a short editorial title to distinguish this work from Rolle's Latin Psalter with commentary.
This is current Oxford catalog wording for the manuscript contents, not an authorial medieval title.
